text: Robotic total gastrectomy for microscopic gastric adenocarcinoma. A forty-year-old female underwent screening EGD for evaluation of epigastric pain. No gastric lesions were seen, and some biopsies were taken throughout the stomach. One of these biopsies returned a signet ring adenocarcinoma.

The exact location of the biopsy was unknown, so she underwent EGD with mapping biopsies in the U.S., including narrowband imaging and methylene blue staining, and no obvious abnormality was identified. This was repeated several months later with the same results. There was no identification of adenocarcinoma. Additional workup included genetic testing for CDH-I mutation, chimerism studies, a CT chest abdomen and pelvis, but despite this, no tumor was able to be localized. After multidisciplinary tumor board discussion, robotic total gastrectomy with DII lymphadenectomy was decided to be this patient's best option.
The surgical setup includes three 8mm ports and one 12mm robotic port, visibly positioned for the robotic total gastrectomy procedure.

The surgeon begins the omentectomy by dissecting the omentum off the transverse colon and entering the lesser sac, proceeding towards the pylorus. A venous branch between the middle colic and right gastroepiploic is divided, followed by the division of the right gastroepiploic pedicle. The dissection transitions to the short gastric vessels. Upon reaching the hiatus, the frontal esophageal ligament is identified, and the left cruce is exposed. The stomach is rolled and retracted laterally to expose and incise the lesser omentum up to the right cruce. The right frontal esophageal ligament is identified, and circumferential dissection of the hiatus is completed. A sponge is placed under the lesser omentum to aid in dissecting the right gastric pedicle, which is divided with a vessel sealer. Dissection continues to the first portion of the duodenum, which is transected two centimeters distal to the pylorus using a blue staple load, and the duodenal stump is over-sewn with 3-0 absorbable barbed suture. The left gastric pedicle is identified and divided using a white staple load. The distal esophagus, mobilized during hiatal dissection, is transected using blue staple loads.

The surgeon completes the DII lymphadenectomy by removing the splenic, celiac, and common hepatic lymph nodes. The anesthesiologist passes the anvil transorally, and the esophageal stump is incised to pull the anvil into the abdomen. The colon is retracted superiorly, and the ligament of Treitz is identified. A loop of jejunum is selected to reach the esophagus without tension. An eight-millimeter trocar is removed, and the incision is enlarged for a small gel port, through which the robotic trocar is reinserted. The jejunum is divided with a white staple load, and the distal limb is incised using monopolar scissors. The circular stapler is inserted through the gel port into the jejunum, brought to the hiatus, and the spike is deployed through the anti-mesenteric border to connect with the anvil, completing the esophagojejunostomy.

The surgeon reinforces the esophagojejunostomy anastomosis with circumferential 2-0 absorbable sutures. The blind end is divided using a white staple load. The bowel is measured 50 centimeters distal to the esophagojejunostomy, and enterotomies are made in the proximal and distal limbs of the jejunum using monopolar scissors. A stapled side-to-side jejunojejunostomy is completed with a white staple load.

The surgeon closes the common channel in two layers, first using a running 2-0 absorbable suture, followed by oversowing with a 2-0 permanent braided suture in a running Lembert fashion. The mesenteric defect is closed using braided permanent suture, and the Peterson defect is closed similarly.

Surgical pathology revealed focal point four centimeter area of residual poorly differentiated signet ring adenocarcinoma arising in the background of chronic gastritis with intestinal metaplasia. Over a hundred and twenty sections of specimen were examined to identify the tumor which was confined to the mucosa. Thirty-one lymph nodes were negative. Final pathologic staging was T-one-A, N-zero, M-zero. Signet-ring adenocarcinoma of the stomach is a very aggressive malignancy, and early diagnosis and treatment offer the best survival. Though this patient had an early-stage gastric cancer, this was a very difficult clinical situation because of the inability to localize the tumor preoperatively. The work of a management required extensive collaboration among multiple disciplines. Though it may seem aggressive performing a total gastrectomy for a four millimeter foci of adenocarcinoma, this was the best option for this patient to provide her with the best overall survival and prognosis.
